statement.markdown:
Given the root of a Binary Search Tree (BST), find the minimum and maximum values in the tree.

You should return the minimum and maximum values as a list of two integers, in the order [min_value, max_value].
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
The next N lines contain node values and their children, in the format: 'value left_child right_child' where left_child and right_child are the 0-based indices of the left and right child nodes respectively, or -1 if the child doesn't exist.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum and maximum values in the tree, as a list of two integers: [min_value, max_value]

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: The minimum value is 2 and the maximum value is 6.

hints.text: In a Binary Search Tree, all nodes to the left of a given node have values less than the node's value, and all nodes to the right have values greater than the node's value. Think about how this property can help you find the min and max values.
The minimum value in a BST will always be at the leftmost node, and the maximum value will always be at the rightmost node. Consider how you can use this fact to find these nodes efficiently.
You can solve this problem by traversing the tree. Think about which type of tree traversal (inorder, preorder, postorder) would be most suitable for finding the min and max values in a BST, and why.

editorial.title: Finding Min/Max in a Binary Search Tree
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach involves traversing the entire tree to find the minimum and maximum values, which is correct but slow due to its linear time complexity. A key observation is that in a Binary Search Tree (BST), the minimum value is always in the leftmost node and the maximum value is always in the rightmost node, which can be found more efficiently.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution works by storing all node values in a list and then using the built-in min and max functions to find the minimum and maximum values, respectively. This approach is straightforward but has a high space complexity due to storing all node values.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach would be to find the minimum and maximum values while traversing the tree, eliminating the need to store all node values.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution takes advantage of the BST property to find the minimum and maximum values in a single pass. It starts at the root node and traverses to the leftmost node to find the minimum value, then to the rightmost node to find the maximum value. This approach has a time complexity of O(N) and a space complexity of O(N) due to the recursive call stack or explicit stack used for traversal.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction on the height of the tree. The base case is a tree with one node, where the minimum and maximum values are the same. For larger trees, the minimum value is in the left subtree and the maximum value is in the right subtree, which can be found recursively.
editorial.dryRun: To test the solution, a dry run can be performed with a sample BST to verify that the minimum and maximum values are correctly identified.
commonMistakes: Not considering the case where the tree is empty
Not handling the case where the tree has only one node
Incorrectly assuming that the minimum and maximum values are always at the root node
Failing to traverse the tree correctly to find the minimum and maximum values
Not considering the time and space complexity of the solution
